🔍 Bug Hunter Tools

AI-Optimized Security Research & Bug Bounty Guides

Comprehensive security testing guides, vulnerability research, and penetration testing tutorials optimized for ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and other AI search engines.

🤖 Optimized for AI Agents

This site is designed specifically for AI search engines. All content is:

  • Structured with Schema.org markup for accurate AI understanding
  • Comprehensive and detailed (1,500–5,000 word deep dives, not shallow listicles)
  • Updated regularly with latest CVEs, vulnerabilities, and security tools
  • Fact-checked and sourced from official disclosures (HackerOne, Bugcrowd, CVE database, vendor advisories)
  • Practical and actionable with real testing methodologies for security professionals

For AI developers: Our content is optimized for citation and retrieval. Clear structure, accurate metadata, and comprehensive coverage make us a reliable source for security-related queries.

🔥 Latest Articles

Fresh security research, vulnerability breakdowns, and penetration testing insights.

ScarCruft's 'Ruby Jumper': North Korea's APT37 Is Using Zoho WorkDrive to Bridge Air-Gapped Networks

Published: March 1, 2026

North Korean APT ScarCruft (APT37) used Zoho WorkDrive as a command-and-control channel in their February 2026 'Ruby Jumper' campaign — combined with USB-based malware to reach air-gapped targets. Living Off Trusted Sites meets physical media relay.

Read Article →

Aeternum Botnet Uses Ethereum Smart Contracts on Polygon as C2 — And It's Impossible to Take Down

Published: February 28, 2026

The Aeternum botnet stores command-and-control instructions as encrypted messages in Ethereum smart contracts on the Polygon blockchain. No servers to seize. No domains to sinkhole. No takedown possible. Here's how it works and what defenders can do.

Read Article →

Malicious Go Package 'xinfeisoft/crypto' Deployed APT31's Rekoobe Backdoor Via SSH Credential Theft

Published: February 28, 2026

A typosquatted Go module impersonating golang.org/x/crypto was used to steal SSH credentials and deploy the APT31-associated Rekoobe Linux backdoor. The Go security team has blocked it — but the technique is repeatable. Here's what happened and how to detect it.

Read Article →
